impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder's conditioning of patent licenses on customers' exclusive purchase of unpatented products constitutes unlawful tying under the Sherman Act when those unpatented products have substantial non-infringing uses.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the jury's finding that Ingevity engaged in unlawful tying by conditioning licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers' exclusive purchase of Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court upheld the antitrust liability and corresponding damages award, affirming that the tied products had substantial non-infringing uses (e.g., air-intake systems outside the patent's scope), defeating Ingevity's patent misuse defense.
impacts.positiveImplications: Competitors benefit from clear precedent that patent holders cannot leverage patent rights to foreclose competition in unpatented product markets when those products have substantial uses beyond the patent's scope.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face significant antitrust liability risk when structuring licensing arrangements that condition patent access on exclusive purchase of unpatented goods, even in adjacent product markets.

impacts.caseName: Trustees of Columbia University v. Gen Digital Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to methods for detecting anomalous program executions by comparing function calls in an emulator to a model are abstract and ineligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101, and whether damages for patent infringement may be based on foreign sales.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated a $185 million jury verdict for Columbia, holding that the asserted cybersecurity patent claims were abstract at step one of Alice and required remand for step two analysis. The court also ruled that the district court erred in allowing damages based on Norton's foreign sales, significantly narrowing the potential damages recovery. The court further vacated enhanced damages and attorneys' fees awards.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies defending against software patent infringement claims benefit from strengthened § 101 eligibility standards for cybersecurity and virus-detection patents, and from the limitation on foreign-sales-based damages.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the cybersecurity and software space face heightened risk that their claims will be found abstract under Alice, and cannot easily recover damages based on worldwide sales of accused products.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether genuine disputes of material fact precluded summary judgment on inequitable conduct in patent prosecution and Walker Process fraud claims for attempted monopolization where the patentee acquired prior art documents before filing patent applications.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgment on both Tenaris's inequitable conduct and Global Tubing's Walker Process antitrust fraud claim, finding genuine disputes of material fact on both issues. Tenaris had acquired prior art documents (CYMAX Documents) describing technology similar to its later-patented BlueCoil product but did not disclose them during prosecution. The case was remanded for trial on both the patent enforceability and antitrust claims.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies accused of inequitable conduct benefit from the court's recognition that factual disputes preclude summary resolution; competitors asserting Walker Process fraud claims similarly benefit from the court's willingness to send such claims to trial.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders who acquire companies and their technical documents face significant risk that failure to disclose those documents during prosecution could support both inequitable conduct defenses and antitrust Walker Process fraud counterclaims.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to a server tailoring content presentation specifications to the capabilities of a requesting wireless device are ineligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed a jury verdict finding Netflix infringed GoTV's streaming media patent, holding on cross-appeal that all asserted claims were patent-ineligible under § 101. The court found the claims directed to the abstract idea of tailoring content delivery to device capabilities. This required reversal of the judgment and entry of judgment for Netflix, eliminating a $2.5 million damages award.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies accused of infringing software and streaming media patents benefit from expanded application of § 101 to invalidate claims directed to content adaptation and delivery optimization.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the streaming media and content delivery space face significant risk that claims directed to adapting content to device capabilities will be deemed abstract and patent-ineligible.

impacts.caseName: Ascendis Pharma A/S v. BioMarin Pharmaceutical Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether an ITC respondent who missed the statutory deadline to seek a mandatory stay of a district court action under 28 U.S.C. § 1659(a)(2) may obtain a stay by voluntarily dismissing and refiling the same declaratory judgment action.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that BioMarin could not circumvent the statutory deadline for seeking a mandatory stay of parallel district court proceedings by dismissing and refiling its declaratory judgment action. The court ruled that allowing such a tactic would undermine the time limits Congress established in § 1659(a) and effectively give ITC respondents unlimited ability to obtain mandatory stays.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent challengers seeking declaratory relief benefit from certainty that ITC respondents cannot manipulate procedural deadlines to indefinitely stay parallel district court proceedings through strategic refiling.
impacts.negativeImplications: ITC respondents who miss the 30-day deadline to seek a mandatory stay of parallel district court litigation cannot cure the deficiency through voluntary dismissal and refiling, limiting strategic options in parallel proceedings.

impacts.caseName: Apple Inc. v. International Trade Commission
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC correctly found that Apple's importation of Apple Watch models with blood oxygen measurement features violated Section 337 of the Tariff Act by infringing Masimo's patents on wearable pulse oximetry technology.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the ITC's finding that Apple violated Section 337 by importing Apple Watch models that infringed Masimo's patents covering wearable blood oxygen measurement devices. The court upheld the Commission's domestic industry determination, validity rulings, and infringement findings, sustaining the limited exclusion order barring importation of the infringing Apple Watches.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders in the medical device and wearable technology space benefit from affirmation that the ITC can effectively enforce patent rights against major technology companies importing infringing consumer products.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies incorporating patented health-monitoring features into consumer electronics face sustained exclusion orders that can disrupt product availability in the U.S. market, with significant revenue and competitive implications.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding a patent damages expert's report and granting summary judgment based on absence of a remedy where the expert used a top-down approach to calculate damages.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of Exafer's damages expert report and vacated the summary judgment for Microsoft based on absence of a remedy. The court found the district court applied too stringent a standard in excluding the expert's top-down damages methodology, effectively denying the patent holder any opportunity to prove damages at trial.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the court's recognition that damages experts should not be excluded merely for using a top-down approach to quantifying infringement damages, preserving flexibility in damages methodologies.
impacts.negativeImplications: Accused infringers may face greater difficulty in obtaining Daubert exclusion of patent damages experts, potentially exposing them to larger damages claims at trial.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding patent infringement expert testimony and granting JMOL of non-infringement in a medical device patent case involving spinal derotation tools.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of two expert witnesses and JMOL for DePuy, finding that the district court improperly excluded testimony on infringement of spinal surgery patents. The case was remanded for a new trial, reinstating claims that DePuy induced surgeons to infringe Barry's patents on en bloc spinal derotation techniques.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the Federal Circuit's closer scrutiny of district court decisions to exclude expert testimony, particularly where exclusion effectively eliminates the entire infringement case.
impacts.negativeImplications: Medical device companies face renewed exposure to induced infringement claims after reversal, with the prospect of a new trial on whether their products induce surgeons to perform patented surgical techniques.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the International Trade Commission's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential in antidumping and countervailing duty investigations is consistent with the governing statute and the common law right of public access to judicial records.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the Court of International Trade's holding that the ITC's blanket practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential is incompatible with the statute and does not abrogate the common law right of access to judicial records. The court treated the Commission's mandamus petition as a notice of appeal under the collateral order doctrine.
impacts.positiveImplications: Importers and domestic industry participants benefit from greater transparency in trade remedy proceedings, with potential access to information previously shielded by the ITC's blanket confidentiality designation.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies submitting questionnaire responses in antidumping and countervailing duty investigations face potential disclosure of business information that was previously automatically treated as confidential, requiring more careful designation of truly confidential data.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTAB correctly construed the claim term 'encryption information' in a patent directed to streaming partly encrypted media content in adaptive bitrate streaming systems.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the PTAB's claim construction in an IPR proceeding involving DivX's patent on streaming encrypted media content. The court adopted a different construction of a key claim term and vacated the Board's decision finding Netflix failed to prove obviousness, remanding for further proceedings under the corrected claim construction. The decision impacts interpretation of patents covering digital rights management in streaming technology.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming and technology companies challenging DRM-related patents in IPR proceedings benefit from the Federal Circuit's willingness to correct PTAB claim construction errors that improperly narrow prior art challenges.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the digital content protection space may face renewed invalidity challenges on remand following correction of claim construction, potentially weakening their enforcement positions.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the PTAB governing discretionary denial of inter partes review petitions constitute a 'general statement of policy' exempt from APA notice-and-comment rulemaking requirements.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions governing discretionary IPR institution decisions are general statements of policy exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking under APA § 553(b). The court held that because Congress gave the Director unreviewable discretion over institution decisions and the instructions do not bind the Director, formal rulemaking was not required.
impacts.positiveImplications: The PTO retains flexibility to issue and revise guidance on IPR institution discretion without the burden of formal rulemaking, preserving administrative efficiency in patent review proceedings.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies seeking to challenge patents through IPR face continued uncertainty about institution standards, as the Director's discretionary denial guidance can be changed without public notice and comment, limiting predictability.

impacts.caseName: 112 Genesee Street, LLC v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Restaurant Revitalization Fund statute created a mandatory, money-mandating obligation enforceable under the Tucker Act, requiring the SBA to award grants to eligible applicants in the order applications were received.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that over 300 restaurants and businesses stated viable claims against the United States for unpaid RRF grants, holding that the statute's directive that the SBA 'shall award grants' in the order received created a money-mandating obligation enforceable under Tucker Act jurisdiction. The court rejected the government's motion to dismiss, allowing the class action to proceed.
impacts.positiveImplications: Small businesses denied federal grant funds under mandatory statutory programs have a viable path to recovery through Tucker Act claims when agencies fail to follow statutory priority rules for disbursement.
impacts.negativeImplications: The federal government faces potential liability for grant programs where statutory language mandates awards in a specified order, limiting agency discretion in fund allocation and creating exposure when appropriated funds are exhausted before all eligible applicants are served.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly approved a $125 million class action settlement resolving claims that the federal judiciary charged excessive PACER fees beyond those permitted by statute.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed approval of the $125 million PACER fee class action settlement, rejecting an objector's challenges to subject matter jurisdiction, settlement fairness, attorneys' fees, and incentive awards. The court upheld the finding that PACER fees had been used to fund expenses unrelated to PACER operations in violation of the E-Government Act, and that the settlement provided meaningful relief to hundreds of thousands of class members.
impacts.positiveImplications: The settlement establishes that the federal judiciary must limit PACER fee usage to costs directly related to PACER operations, potentially reducing future fees for businesses and law firms that regularly access court records electronically.

impacts.caseName: Fortress Iron, LP v. Digger Specialties, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patents are invalid for omission of a coinventor when the patent holder acknowledges the coinventor's contributions but cannot locate the coinventor to obtain consent for correction under 35 U.S.C. § 256.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that two patents were invalid for omitting a coinventor, rejecting the patent holder's argument that it should be allowed to correct inventorship under § 256(b) despite being unable to locate the missing coinventor. The court held that the inability to find the coinventor did not excuse the inventorship defect or authorize judicial correction without the coinventor's participation.
impacts.positiveImplications: Accused infringers can effectively defend against patents where coinventors were omitted, even when the omission was unintentional, creating a powerful invalidity defense in cases involving collaborative development with foreign partners.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies that collaborate with foreign manufacturers or partners on product development face significant risk that failure to properly identify and name all coinventors—particularly those who later become unreachable—can render resulting patents invalid and unenforceable.
